food_subgroup: Spiced black tea brewed with milk and sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Masala Chai. Nearest parent is a spiced milk tea beverage. A new concept for South Asian masala chai preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Spiced black tea with milk (masala chai) (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of masala chai is 1 cup (approximately 200ml / 210g) — a warm, amber-coloured, milky, sweet and spicy tea with a strong ginger-cardamom fragrance.

nutrient_source.title: Masala Chai — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from standard masala chai recipe component data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone masala chai entry. Values derived from ICMR-NIN data for full-fat milk, sugar, black tea (negligible calories), and standard spice blend. Masala chai is approximately 50% milk, 40% water, 8% sugar, 2% spices/tea by weight.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from sweetened milk tea; the milk protein moderates the GI of the added sugar; spices have negligible glycaemic effect.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 67mg per cup (210g) — very low. Masala chai is one of the lowest-sodium warm beverages. The calcium and potassium from milk support blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Suitable for hypertensive patients — very low sodium. Limit added sugar. Ginger in masala chai may have modest vasodilatory effects benefiting blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~42); glycaemic load per cup (210g) is approximately 9. The sugar content (8.8g/100g) provides 18.5g of sugar per cup.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should request masala chai with less sugar or without sugar (the spice flavour is pleasant without sugar). The cinnamon in masala chai may improve insulin sensitivity.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper all have documented anti-inflammatory properties; the combination is one of the most potent anti-inflammatory spice blends in culinary use.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Masala chai is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the ginger, cinnamon, and cloves provide potent anti-inflammatory compounds. Ginger's gingerols and cinnamon's cinnamaldehyde have documented benefits for joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Chai is India's national warming beverage — black tea brewed directly with whole aromatic spices (fresh ginger, green cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper) in water, then full-fat milk and sugar added and the whole mixture simmered together for 2–3 minutes before straining. The result is a rich, amber-coloured, milky, intensely fragrant tea with a warming, spicy kick. The spice blend varies by region and household — ginger and cardamom are universal, while cinnamon, cloves, and pepper are common additions. Available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and teh tarik counters across Singapore. Provides notable anti-inflammatory spice compounds.

confidence_reason: No standalone masala chai entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN full-fat milk and sugar data per standard recipe proportions (~50% milk, 40% water, 8% sugar). Milk and sugar composition are well-established; spice contribution is negligible.
notes: Contains dairy (milk). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 12mg per cup (210g). Caffeine content approximately 30–50mg per cup from black tea. Values assume 1.5 teaspoons sugar per cup (standard Indian chai sweetness). The anti-inflammatory spice compounds (gingerols, cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, piperine) are bioactive but contribute negligible macronutrients at standard brewing concentrations.
